2604.16116.title: The Relic Condition: When Published Scholarship Becomes Material for Its Own Replacement
2604.16116.paper_summary: We extracted the scholarly reasoning systems of two internationally prominent humanities and social science scholars from their published corpora alone, converted those systems into structured inference-time constraints for a large language model, and tested whether the resulting scholar-bots could perform core academic functions at expert-assessed quality. The distillation pipeline used an eight-layer extraction method and a nine-module skill architecture grounded in local, closed-corpus analysis. The scholar-bots were then deployed across doctoral supervision, peer review, lecturing and panel-style academic exchange. Expert assessment involved three senior academics producing reports and appointment-level syntheses. Across the preserved expert record, all review and supervision reports judged the outputs benchmark-attaining, appointment-level recommendations placed both bots at or above Senior Lecturer level in the Australian university system, and recovered panel scores placed Scholar A between 7.9 and 8.9/10 and Scholar B between 8.5 and 8.9/10 under multi-turn debate conditions. A research-degree-student survey showed high performance ratings across information reliability, theoretical depth and logical rigor, with pronounced ceiling effects on a 7-point scale, despite all participants already being frontier-model users. We term this the Relic condition: when publication systems make stable reasoning architectures legible, extractable and cheaply deployable, the public record of intellectual labor becomes raw material for its own functional replacement. Because the technical threshold for this transition is already crossed at modest engineering effort, we argue that the window for protective frameworks covering disclosure, consent, compensation and deployment restriction is the present, while deployment remains optional rather than infrastructural.
2604.16116.paper_summary_zh: 我們僅從兩位國際知名的人文與社會科學學者的已發表文獻中提取了他們的學術推理系統，將這些系統轉換為大型語言模型的結構化推理時間約束，並測試所產生的學者機器人是否能以專家評估的質量執行核心學術功能。提煉流程使用了八層提取方法和九模塊技能架構，基於本地的封閉語料庫分析。然後，學者機器人被部署於博士生指導、同行評審、講座和小組式學術交流中。專家評估涉及三位資深學者產生報告和任命級別的綜合評析。在保留的專家記錄中，所有的評審和指導報告都認為輸出達到基準水平，任命級別的推薦將兩個機器人評定為澳大利亞大學系統中的高級講師級別或以上，而在多輪辯論條件下，學者A的得分介於7.9至8.9/10之間，學者B的得分介於8.5至8.9/10之間。一項研究生調查顯示，在信息可靠性、理論深度和邏輯嚴謹性方面的表現評分很高，在7分制上出現了明顯的天花板效應，儘管所有參與者已經是前沿模型的使用者。我們將此稱為遺跡條件：當出版系統使穩定的推理架構變得可讀、可提取且廉價可部署時，智力勞動的公共記錄成為其自身功能替代的原材料。由於這一過渡的技術門檻已在適度的工程努力下被跨越，我們主張，涵蓋披露、同意、補償和部署限制的保護框架的窗口是當前的，儘管部署仍然是可選的，而非基礎設施性的。
2604.16116.author: Lin Deng et.al.
